terms.definition: A single Python file (.py) containing definitions and code that can be imported and reused elsewhere.
A directory of modules treated as a single importable unit; a regular package contains an __init__.py.
The statement (import / from ... import) that loads a module and binds names from it into the current namespace.
The `from module import name` form that binds specific names directly, so they can be used without the module prefix.
A mapping from names to objects. Each module has its own namespace, which keeps names from different modules from colliding.
A file that marks a directory as a regular package; it runs on import and can expose or initialize the package's public API.
The `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard, which runs code only when the file is executed directly rather than imported.
An import that names the full path from a top-level package, e.g. from myapp.clients import client. Generally preferred for clarity.
An import relative to the current package using leading dots, e.g. from .clients import client. Usable only inside a package.
The list of locations (sys.path) Python searches to find a module: the script directory, PYTHONPATH and installed site-packages. The first match wins.
Python imports each module once and stores it in sys.modules; later imports return the cached object, so top-level code runs a single time.
